findings.location: Metabox_Taxonomy::save() line 135-136; Common::sanitize_hfc_data() lines 521-526; Common::sanitize_html_with_scripts() lines 447-497
findings.reachability: Any WordPress user holding the edit_terms capability for a registered taxonomy (typically Editor or above, sometimes lower on custom taxonomies) can submit the taxonomy metabox form and store arbitrary JavaScript via <script> tags in the head/body/footer fields. The stored code is then rendered unescaped into every page of that taxonomy archive via class-front.php inject_code().
findings.justification: sanitize_hfc_data() intentionally passes head/body/footer through sanitize_html_with_scripts(), which explicitly preserves full <script> and <style> block content (only opening-tag attributes are sanitized). The saved data is later echoed raw in class-front.php line 152-155 with the comment 'We do not use wp_kses because that would escape < > &'. This is the plugin's core feature and is documented, but the capability gate (edit_terms) is lower than unfiltered_html, meaning Editors can inject JS that fires for all site visitors on taxonomy pages.

detection_pattern.section_name: Stored XSS: script injection permitted below unfiltered_html capability
detection_pattern.description: Catches plugins that deliberately allow <script> tags in stored content without gating on unfiltered_html capability. Users below Administrator level can inject JavaScript that executes for all site visitors.

findings.reachability: Internal code path only; not directly exploitable.
findings.justification: Line 97 returns early if $_POST['auhfc'] is not set. Lines 129-132 re-check the same condition and call delete_term_meta(), but this branch can never be reached because execution would have already returned at line 97-99. The delete logic is dead code and may represent a missing or misapplied feature (e.g., explicit empty-submission to delete the meta). If the intent was to allow deletion by submitting without the 'auhfc' key, the early-return at line 97 prevents it.

detection_pattern.section_name: Dead code: unreachable delete branch after early-return guard
detection_pattern.description: Finds term/post meta deletion calls that may be dead code due to earlier isset() guards on the same POST key. Indicates unintended feature omission or logic error in save handlers.

findings.reachability: Internally called from form rendering; visible to any user who can view the taxonomy edit form.
findings.justification: get_security_risk_notice() returns an associative array with keys 'title' and 'message', but print_security_risk_notice() passes that array directly to wp_kses() which expects a string. PHP will cast the array to the literal string 'Array', so the notice is never rendered correctly. This is a bug that silently swallows the security warning, not a direct security vulnerability, but demonstrates lack of test coverage and could mask future issues.

detection_pattern.section_name: Type confusion: array passed to wp_kses() expecting string
detection_pattern.description: Catches calls where wp_kses() receives a non-string value (e.g., a method return that is an array). The function silently converts the array to 'Array', defeating any intended security notice or output.

findings.reachability: Only reachable after passing the in_array() allowlist check at line 105, so attacker-controlled taxonomy is constrained to plugin-configured values.
findings.justification: The nonce field name and action are derived from $_POST['taxonomy'] (line 102) via get_nonce_name() and get_nonce_action(). While in_array() on line 105 constrains $taxonomy to plugin-configured values before the nonce lookup on line 110, the nonce key used to READ from $_POST is built from that already-sanitized $taxonomy. Because in_array() runs before the nonce read, the risk is neutralised in practice, but the ordering means a future refactor that moves the in_array() check could expose nonce-key injection.

detection_pattern.section_name: Nonce key derived from unvalidated POST field
detection_pattern.description: Catches nonce verification flows where the nonce field name or action string is constructed from a POST parameter before that parameter is allowlist-validated. Attacker can choose which nonce field is read, potentially bypassing verification if allowlist check is absent or reordered.
